title: Spaghetti aglio e olio
subtitle: Spaghetti with garlic, olive oil, and chilli
excerpt: Spaghetti tossed in olive oil infused with slowly cooked garlic and chilli, finished with parsley and pasta water. The midnight pasta of Naples, six ingredients, fifteen minutes, and a technique that turns pasta water and oil into a glossy coating sauce.
sourceNotes: Based on the Neapolitan spaghetti aglio e olio tradition as documented in Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992) and Anna Del Conte, 'Gastronomy of Italy' (1987). This is one of the oldest preparations in the Italian pasta repertoire, common across southern Italy but particularly associated with Naples.

content.text: Aglio e olio is a late-night kitchen preparation, what Italian cooks make when the fridge is empty and there is spaghetti in the cupboard. Six ingredients, no sauce to prepare in advance. The technique reduces to one critical point: the garlic must colour gently, turning ivory, without burning. Burnt garlic cannot be rescued and will make the entire dish bitter.

The pasta water is the second key element. A generous ladle of starchy water added to the pan with the drained pasta, tossed vigorously, creates an emulsion between the oil and the water that coats the spaghetti in a light, glossy sauce. Without pasta water, the oil pools at the bottom of the bowl.

items.name: Chilli flakes
items.prepNote: or 1 fresh red chilli, sliced thin

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over the lowest possible heat. Add {{garlic}} very thinly sliced garlic and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli. Cook slowly for 8 to 10 minutes, swirling occasionally, until the garlic is pale ivory with golden edges. It should never darken past light gold. Remove from the heat.

Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a large mug of pasta water. Drain the pasta and add directly to the garlic pan, the pan does not need to be back on the heat yet. Toss to coat in the oil. Return to a low heat and add a large ladleful of pasta water. Toss vigorously for 1 to 2 minutes until an emulsion forms and the pasta is glossy. Add {{parsley}} of chopped parsley, toss once more, and serve immediately.

items.fix: Start again, burnt garlic cannot be remedied. Use the lowest possible heat and take 8 to 10 minutes for this step.
items.symptom: Garlic is bitter and dark brown

items.fix: Add more pasta water and toss hard for 2 full minutes. The emulsion forms only through active agitation.
items.cause: Not enough pasta water, or tossing not vigorous enough
items.symptom: Pasta is oily rather than coated in an emulsion

items.fix: The pasta water should taste clearly salty. Six garlic cloves for four people is not too much, this dish depends on an assertive garlic flavour.
items.cause: Pasta water not salty enough, or too little garlic

content.text: Aglio e olio is the pasta of the Naples midnight kitchen and the Italian student diet. The appeal is the simplicity. You need nothing the pantry doesn't already hold. The trick is treating pasta water as an ingredient, not waste. It is a baseline dish in the Italian repertoire. Any cook who claims to know Italian food should be able to make it well. There is nowhere for the cooking to hide.
